skill.name: face-swap
skill.description: Route face-swap and character-swap requests to the right RunComfy CLI workflow for still images, video, motion transfer, and batch edits.
skill.summary: Use RunComfy CLI routes for consent-based face swaps in images and videos.
skill.author: runcomfy-com

security_audit.summary: The static command findings are mostly Markdown code spans and documented runcomfy CLI examples, not arbitrary shell execution. The confirmed risks are legitimate but elevated: the skill runs a networked media-generation CLI, sends user-provided media URLs to RunComfy APIs, stores an API token file, and supports dual-use face-swap workflows that require consent controls.

medium_findings.title: Dual-use face-swap workflow requires consent controls
medium_findings.description: The skill explicitly supports face and character replacement, including deepfake-style requests. It documents consent and refusal guidance, but it does not enforce those rules before API execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The semantic risk is clear from the face-swap purpose and the consent warning. The file also states that the skill itself does not gate inputs.

medium_findings.title: Networked CLI execution with user media URLs
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to call runcomfy run with image, audio, and video URLs. The CLI posts jobs to a model API and downloads generated outputs from RunComfy domains.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tool declaration and command examples confirm external CLI use. The workflow is bounded to runcomfy, so it is elevated risk rather than arbitrary command execution.

medium_findings.title: API token stored in a hidden home configuration file
medium_findings.description: The skill documents that runcomfy login writes an API token to a hidden file under the user home directory. The stated file mode reduces exposure but still creates credential-handling risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token path and permissions are explicitly documented. This is legitimate CLI behavior, but marketplace users should understand local credential storage.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs are product and documentation links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings point to RunComfy home, model catalog, documentation, example CDN placeholders, and related skill links. No evidence found of covert exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation links or placeholder asset URLs. They align with the skill purpose and do not include hidden payload transport.

low_findings.title: Weak-crypto and reconnaissance alerts are Markdown false positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography and reconnaissance lines are prose, route descriptions, model names, or exit-code documentation. No evidence found of cryptographic code or system inventory collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review of the cited regions shows documentation text only. There are no hashing calls, crypto libraries, host probes, or network scanning commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: Allowed Bash scope for RunComfy CLI commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill grants Bash access limited to runcomfy commands and provides several runcomfy run examples. This is expected for the skill but should remain scoped.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools metadata and command examples are direct evidence. The scope is constrained to runcomfy, reducing command injection risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Third-party media URLs are treated as untrusted inputs
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill accepts external image, audio, and video URLs as model inputs and warns that reference assets are untrusted. This creates privacy and indirect prompt-injection exposure.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples use external media URLs and the security section names reference assets as untrusted. The mitigation is documented but not enforced by the skill.

content.value_statement: Face-swap requests need the right route for still images, videos, motion transfer, or batches. This skill selects RunComfy workflows and gives consent-aware CLI guidance.
actual_capabilities: Routes video character swaps to Wan 2-2 Animate or Kling Motion Control.
Routes still image swaps to GPT Image 2 Edit, Nano Banana Edit, or Flux Kontext.
Provides RunComfy CLI command patterns for each supported model route.
Explains when to choose single-shot, batch, motion-preserving, or identity-preserving workflows.
Documents consent, disclosure, token storage, endpoint boundaries, and output download behavior.
limitations: Requires the RunComfy CLI, a RunComfy account, and valid authentication.
Sends user-provided media URLs to RunComfy model APIs for processing.
Does not enforce consent, identity rights, or platform disclosure rules automatically.
Some ComfyUI workflow variants are not reachable through these CLI routes.

use_cases.title: Consent-based campaign stills
use_cases.description: Place an approved spokesperson face into a product scene while preserving pose, lighting, clothing, and background.

use_cases.title: Character motion transfer
use_cases.description: Transfer motion from a reference performance onto an approved target character for a short video concept.

use_cases.title: Batch identity consistency
use_cases.description: Apply one approved identity across multiple product or lifestyle images with consistent aspect ratio and resolution.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Still Image Route
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the face-swap skill for one still image. I have consent for both images. Preserve pose, lighting, clothing, and background.

prompt_templates.title: Swap Across a Batch
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the face-swap skill for a batch. Use the first image as the approved identity reference and apply it to the remaining scenes.

prompt_templates.title: Preserve Source Motion
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the face-swap skill for video motion transfer. I have rights to the performance video and the target character image.

prompt_templates.title: Select the Best Model
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare the RunComfy face-swap routes for my assets. Recommend the best model based on still versus video, batch size, and motion needs.

output_examples.input: A user asks to swap an approved headshot into one campaign image.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends GPT Image 2 Edit, assigns source and identity roles, and emphasizes preserving pose, clothing, lighting, and background.

output_examples.input: A user has one approved identity reference and several product scenes.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends Nano Banana Edit for batch consistency and advises locking aspect ratio and resolution across the set.

output_examples.input: A user wants a stylized character to perform motion from a source clip.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends Kling Motion Control Pro because the task depends on preserving reference motion rather than only replacing a face.

best_practices: Confirm identity rights, source media rights, and platform disclosure requirements before running any route.
Use clear reference ordering and preservation instructions for still image swaps.
Keep RunComfy tokens scoped, private, and out of shared logs or prompts.
anti_patterns: Do not create face swaps of real people without consent.
Do not use the skill for defamatory, sexual, deceptive, or impersonation content.
Do not pipe remote install scripts into a shell to set up the CLI.

faq.question: Does this skill perform the face swap locally?
faq.answer: No. It routes requests through the RunComfy CLI and RunComfy model APIs.

faq.question: Can it handle both images and videos?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents separate RunComfy routes for still images, video character swaps, and motion transfer.

faq.question: Does it verify consent automatically?
faq.answer: No. The operator must confirm consent and refuse unsafe or non-consensual requests.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but it runs a networked CLI and enables sensitive synthetic media workflows.

faq.question: Where is the RunComfy token stored?
faq.answer: The skill states that runcomfy login stores the token in a hidden config file with restrictive permissions.
